section.title: Into Your Hands I Commit My Spirit
section.sectionRationale: A psalm of David combining urgent lament with settled trust, its central confession, 'Into Your hands I commit my spirit,' becoming the very words Jesus prayed from the cross, Luke 23:46. David's distress, betrayal by friends, and grief give way to praise for God's goodness and a closing call to be strong and take heart.
section.thesis: David commits his life into God's hands amid betrayal, grief, and mortal danger, and that same confession becomes the dying prayer of Jesus Christ, who entrusted His spirit to the Father and was raised in vindication of that trust.
section.frameNote: Five movements: trust and the commit of his spirit into God's hands (1-5); continued confidence and rejection of worthless idols (6-8); distress, grief, and betrayal by friends (9-13); renewed trust despite distress and prayer against the wicked (14-18); and praise for God's goodness, closing with a call to be strong and take heart (19-24).

units.label: Into Your hands I commit my spirit
units.note: trust and the commit of his spirit

verses.text:
In You, O LORD, I have {{chasah|taken refuge}}; let me never be put to shame; 
save me by Your righteousness.
Incline Your ear to me; come quickly to my rescue. Be my rock of refuge, the stronghold of my deliverance.
For You are my rock and my fortress; lead me and guide me for the sake of Your name.
You free me from the net laid out for me, for You are my refuge.
Into Your hands I commit my {{ruach|spirit}}; You have redeemed me, O LORD, God of {{emet|truth}}.
I hate those who cling to worthless idols, but in the LORD I trust.

I will be glad and rejoice in Your loving devotion, for You have seen my affliction; 
You have known the anguish of my soul.
You have not delivered me to the enemy; You have set my feet in the open.

units.label: I am dreaded by my friends

verses.text: Be merciful to me, O LORD, for I am in distress; my eyes fail from sorrow, my soul and body as well.
For my life is consumed with grief and my years with groaning; my iniquity has drained my strength, and my bones are wasting away.
Among all my enemies I am a disgrace, and among my neighbors even more. I am dreaded by my friends, they flee when they see me on the street.
I am forgotten like a dead man, out of mind. I am like a broken vessel.
For I hear the slander of many; there is terror on every side. They conspire against me and plot to take my life.

units.label: My times are in Your hands

verses.text: But I trust in You, O LORD; I say, "You are my God."

My times are in Your hands; deliver me from my enemies 
and from those who pursue me.
Make Your face shine on Your servant; save me by Your loving devotion.
O LORD, let me not be ashamed, for I have called on You. Let the wicked be put to shame; let them lie silent in Sheol.

May lying lips be silenced, lips that speak with arrogance against the righteous, 
full of pride and contempt.

units.label: Blessed be the LORD
units.note: praise and a closing call to courage

verses.text: How great is Your goodness which You have laid up for those who fear You, which You have bestowed before the sons of men on those who take refuge in You!
You hide them in the secret place of Your presence from the schemes of men. You conceal them in Your shelter from accusing tongues.

Blessed be the LORD, for He has shown me His loving devotion 
in a city under siege.
In my alarm I said, "I am cut off from Your sight!" But You heard my plea for mercy when I called to You for help.

Love the LORD, all His saints. The LORD preserves the faithful, 
but fully repays the arrogant.
Be strong and courageous, all you who hope in the LORD.

chasah.gloss: To take refuge, seek shelter, as a bird under wings or a traveler in a fortress. It describes an active, ongoing trust rather than a single act, the posture David maintains throughout the psalm even amid betrayal and danger.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, breath, the animating life given by God. David commits this very spirit into God's hands, words that become the dying prayer of Jesus on the cross, 'Father, into Your hands I commit My spirit,' Luke 23:46.

emet.gloss: Truth, faithfulness, reliability. David appeals to God as the God of emet, whose redemption can be trusted absolutely, the same faithfulness fully displayed when God raised Jesus from the dead in vindication of His trust.

basic.context: David takes refuge in the LORD as rock and fortress, committing his very spirit into God's hands, confident in the God of truth who redeems him. Yet the psalm turns to raw distress: grief, physical wasting, disgrace before enemies and neighbors, and even dread among his own friends who flee at the sight of him. He renews his trust, declaring that his times are in God's hands, prays against lying and arrogant accusers, and closes in praise for God's goodness hidden away for those who take refuge in Him, urging all who hope in the LORD to be strong and courageous.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm should be read as an integrated whole in which trust does not eliminate grief but is proven precisely in the middle of betrayal and distress; its central confession, verse 5, carries weight far beyond David's own life because Jesus Himself prayed these very words while dying.
basic.originalLanguages: David has chasah, taken refuge, in the LORD, an ongoing posture rather than a single decision, and commits his ruach, spirit, into God's hands, appealing to Him as the God of emet, truth and faithfulness, whose redemption can be utterly relied upon even at the point of death.
basic.typology: David's commitment of his spirit into God's hands, verse 5, becomes, without alteration, the dying words of Jesus on the cross, Luke 23:46, and David's experience of being forgotten like a dead man and like a broken vessel, verse 12, anticipates the humiliation and apparent abandonment of the suffering Messiah, who was nonetheless vindicated by resurrection.
basic.christ: Jesus prays this psalm's central verse word for word as He dies: 'Father, into Your hands I commit My spirit,' Luke 23:46, taking up David's confession of trust as His own final act of faith before death. Where David was delivered from death's threat, Jesus actually died and was raised, the God of emet, truth, proving faithful not merely to preserve life but to conquer death itself, vindicating every word of trust this psalm contains, Acts 2:24.
basic.god: God is shown as a faithful refuge who sees affliction, knows the anguish of the soul, and can be trusted utterly even at the point of death, His goodness hidden away like treasure for those who take refuge in Him and displayed openly before the world in due time.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 23:46 records Jesus praying the exact words of verse 5 as He dies on the cross, the ultimate fulfillment of this psalm's central trust. Acts 2:24 declares that God raised Jesus, freeing Him from the agony of death, because it was not possible for death to hold Him, the vindication of trust this psalm anticipates. Romans 8:31-39 echoes this psalm's confidence that nothing, not even death, can separate believers from the love of God found in Christ Jesus.
basic.oneStory: David's confession, 'Into Your hands I commit my spirit,' spoken amid grief, betrayal, and mortal danger, becomes the very prayer Jesus prays while dying on the cross, and just as David trusted the God of truth for redemption, the Father raised Jesus from the dead, proving that trust in God's hands is never in vain, even at the point of death.

context.literary: A psalm of David combining confident trust, verses 1-8, raw lament, verses 9-13, renewed trust and imprecation, verses 14-18, and closing praise with exhortation, verses 19-24, a pattern showing the full emotional range of genuine faith held together in a single prayer.
context.historical: Traditionally attributed to David amid betrayal by friends and plots against his life, possibly during Saul's pursuit or Absalom's rebellion, both seasons marked by disgrace before enemies and painful abandonment by those closest to him.
context.canonical: Its central verse becomes uniquely significant in the Gospels as the last words of Jesus from the cross, Luke 23:46, connecting this psalm of individual trust and suffering directly to the passion narrative and the Christian hope of resurrection.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To model trust that commits one's whole life, even one's spirit at the point of death, into God's hands, honestly processing grief and betrayal while still praising God's goodness and urging others to be strong and courageous in hope.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific experience of distress and trust; it functions prescriptively as a pattern of prayer for believers facing betrayal, grief, or mortal danger, and its central verse becomes uniquely, directly prescriptive as the very prayer Jesus prayed while dying.

debates.question: When Jesus prays the words of Psalm 31:5 from the cross, is He fulfilling a specific messianic prophecy embedded in the psalm, or appropriating a well-known Jewish prayer of trust that any pious Israelite might use at the point of death?

views.label: Jesus's use of this verse is a deliberate messianic fulfillment, since the whole psalm's pattern of betrayal by friends, disgrace, and vindicated trust matches His passion so closely that its use at the cross reveals David's words as prophetically pointing beyond himself to the greater Son of David
views.case: The specific details of the psalm, betrayal by close friends, being like a broken and forgotten vessel, surrounded by conspiracy and slander, correspond remarkably to the Gospel passion accounts, suggesting more than coincidental reuse of a phrase.

views.label: Jewish tradition used Psalm 31:5 as a customary evening prayer of committal, so Jesus's use of it may simply reflect a devout Israelite praying a familiar, fitting prayer of trust at the moment of death, without every detail of the psalm necessarily being direct prophecy
views.case: The verse's use in Jewish evening liturgy as a prayer said before sleep, entrusting oneself to God, suggests Jesus may have been drawing on a common and appropriate prayer rather than intentionally signaling a predictive prophecy in every phrase of the psalm.

debates.bearing: Either way, Jesus's use of David's words at the cross shows this psalm's trust reaching its fullest and most consequential expression in Him, whether by direct prophetic design or by a perfectly fitting prayer of faith at the point of death; both readings affirm that the psalm's confidence is finally, completely vindicated in Christ's resurrection.

study.originalLanguages: David has chasah, taken refuge, in the LORD as an ongoing posture, and commits his ruach, spirit, into God's hands, appealing to Him as the God of emet, truth and faithfulness, whose redeeming reliability is proven completely when God raises Jesus, who prays these same words, from the dead.

typology.type: Committing one's spirit into God's hands, verse 5
typology.fulfillment: Jesus's dying words on the cross, 'Father, into Your hands I commit My spirit,' Luke 23:46.

typology.type: Being forgotten like a dead man, like a broken vessel, verse 12
typology.fulfillment: The humiliation of the suffering Messiah, vindicated by God's raising Him from the dead, Acts 2:24.

study.christ: Jesus prays this psalm's central words exactly as He dies, 'Father, into Your hands I commit My spirit,' Luke 23:46, making David's confession of trust His own final act of faith before death. David was delivered from the threat of death; Jesus entered death fully and was raised by the Father, the God of emet, truth, who proved faithful not merely to preserve a threatened life but to conquer death itself, Acts 2:24, vindicating every word of trust this psalm contains for all who are united to Him.
study.god: God is shown as a faithful refuge who sees affliction and knows the soul's anguish intimately, whose goodness is stored up like hidden treasure for those who take refuge in Him, trustworthy enough to receive a life, or a spirit, committed into His hands even at the point of death.

crossReferences.note: Jesus prays the exact words of verse 5 while dying on the cross, the ultimate fulfillment of this psalm's central confession of trust.
God raises Jesus, freeing Him from death's agony since it was not possible for death to hold Him, the final vindication of this psalm's trust.
Nothing can separate believers from the love of God in Christ, echoing this psalm's confidence that God's hands are the safest place to entrust one's life.

study.oneStory: David's confession, 'Into Your hands I commit my spirit,' spoken amid grief, betrayal, and mortal danger, becomes the very prayer Jesus prays while dying on the cross. Just as David trusted the God of truth for redemption, the Father raised Jesus from the dead, proving that a life, or a spirit, committed into God's hands is never lost.

sermon.title: "My Times Are in Your Hand" - Part One (Biola)
sermon.why: Begg preaches on Psalm 31:15, showing that to say 'my times are in Your hand' means we are not trapped by blind fate or tossed about by chance, but are being schooled in the providence of a God who is making us like His Son.

quiz.q: What does David commit into God's hands in verse 5, and who later prays these exact words?

options.text: His spirit; Jesus prays these words while dying on the cross, Luke 23:46.
options.note: This is the psalm's central confession and its direct New Testament fulfillment.

options.text: His spirit; Stephen later echoes similar words while being stoned, Acts 7:59.
options.note: Close, since Stephen does pray something similar as he dies, but the psalm's words are quoted verbatim by Jesus Himself, Luke 23:46, which is the direct fulfillment in view here.

options.text: His kingdom; Solomon later prays this at the temple dedication.
options.note: The verse is about David's own spirit, not his kingdom, and is fulfilled by Jesus, not Solomon.

options.text: His army; Joshua later prays this before battle.
options.note: The verse concerns committing one's spirit at the point of vulnerability, not military strategy.

quiz.q: How does David describe his experience among his friends in verse 11?

options.text: He is dreaded by his friends, who flee when they see him in the street.
options.note: This verse describes painful abandonment even by those closest to him.

options.text: His friends grow distant and stop visiting, though they do not actively flee.
options.note: Close, since growing distance is part of the picture, but the verse describes something more dramatic, friends fleeing at the very sight of him in the street.

options.text: His friends betray him but never physically avoid him.
options.note: The text specifically describes friends fleeing at the sight of him in the street, more than passive betrayal.

options.text: He has no friends to speak of at all.
options.note: The verse implies he did have friends, who then dreaded and avoided him, not that he never had any.

quiz.q: What confession does David make in verse 14 despite his distress?

options.text: "But I trust in You, O LORD; I say, 'You are my God.'"
options.note: This marks the psalm's renewed confession of trust in the middle of ongoing distress.

options.text: "I hope God is still with me, though I am not certain."
options.note: Close in tone, since the psalm includes real uncertainty elsewhere, but verse 14 itself is a direct, confident confession, 'You are my God,' not a tentative hope.

options.text: "I will seek help from foreign gods instead."
options.note: David explicitly rejects worthless idols elsewhere in the psalm, verse 6, and reaffirms trust in the LORD alone.

options.text: "I demand that God explain my suffering."
options.note: The verse is a confession of trust, not a demand for explanation.

quiz.q: What does verse 15 say about David's times?

options.text: They are in God's hands.
options.note: This phrase grounds David's confidence that his circumstances, not just his spirit, are held securely by God.

options.text: They are generally watched over by God, though not specifically controlled by Him.
options.note: Close, since God's care is the point, but the verse states directly and specifically that David's times 'are in Your hands,' active possession, not general, hands-off watching.

options.text: They are determined by his enemies.
options.note: The verse specifically attributes control to God, even though enemies and pursuers are mentioned in the same breath.

options.text: They are entirely unknown even to God.
options.note: The verse affirms God's active possession of David's times, not divine ignorance of them.

quiz.q: What is the psalm's closing exhortation in verse 24?

options.text: Be strong and courageous, all who hope in the LORD.
options.note: The psalm ends by extending its personal testimony into an exhortation for the whole community of faith.

options.text: Be patient and calm, without any call to active courage.
options.note: Close, since patience is implied by hoping in the LORD, but verse 24 explicitly calls for active strength and courage, not merely passive calm.

options.text: Seek vengeance personally against all enemies.
options.note: The psalm prays for God's justice against the wicked, verses 17-18, but the closing exhortation is about courage and hope, not personal vengeance.

options.text: Withdraw from community and suffer alone.
options.note: The exhortation addresses 'all who hope in the LORD,' a communal call, not a call to isolation.

questions: David commits his very spirit into God's hands, words Jesus later prayed while dying. What would it mean for you to entrust not just your circumstances but your very life into God's hands today?
David experiences the pain of friends fleeing from him in his distress. Have you ever felt abandoned by people you expected to stand by you, and how did that shape your trust in God?
Despite deep distress, David renews his confession, 'You are my God,' and declares that his times are in God's hands. What present uncertainty in your life most needs this same renewed confession?

Jesus's use of this psalm's words on the cross shows that even His darkest hour was held within a psalm of trust, not despair alone. How does knowing Jesus prayed these very words change how you read your own hardest prayers?
The psalm closes urging all who hope in the LORD to be strong and courageous. Where do you need that courage this week, and who around you might also need to hear this exhortation from you?

translationNotes.note: Into Your hands I commit my spirit was used in later Jewish tradition as an evening prayer of committal before sleep, and is quoted by Jesus without alteration as His last words from the cross, Luke 23:46.
The BSB text originally rendered this verse with a dash marking a pause before 'they flee'; it is rendered here as a comma for consistency, with no change to meaning.
Cut off from Your sight reflects a moment of despair within the psalm, immediately answered by the testimony that God heard his plea, showing that feelings of abandonment are not the final word.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 31:1-24. The BSB source text contained one em dash in verse 11, converted to a comma per house style; no em or en dashes remain in this file. Sermon link verified this session via web_fetch of truthforlife.org.
